summary: Spolsky discusses research from the Journal of Experimental Psychology that supports his long-held claim that software developers cannot effectively multitask. He argues that task switching between projects incurs massive overhead, and that developers assigned to multiple simultaneous projects produce far less than if they worked sequentially. The real reason managers allow multitasking despite its productivity cost is that it obscures unrealistic schedules — working on three projects at once creates the illusion they'll all finish in a month. In reality, the work takes far longer and some tasks may never get completed at all.
key_points: Academic research confirms that rapid task switching incurs significant cognitive overhead, even beyond what the study directly measured.
Developers alternating between two major projects, even on different days, produce less total output than working sequentially.
Teams often take weeks to reach full productivity when starting a new project after finishing an old one.
Managers assign simultaneous projects not because it's efficient, but because it hides the fact that the schedule is unrealistic.
Multitasking creates an illusion of parallel progress while actually extending timelines dramatically.
money_quotes: The overhead of task switching can be huge.
Developers who have two major projects to work on, even if they alternate every other day, will not get as much done as if they do them sequentially.
Despite the fact that the multitasking dramatically reduces productivity, they're willing to do it because it obscures the schedule.

It seems like each of those tasks takes about a month. Do them in sequence, and three months will elapse. Do them at the same time, and it's possible to deceive yourself that only a month will elapse.
In reality, six months will elapse and one task won't even get done.
This just proves that the schedule is completely unrealistic.
key_insight: Multitasking doesn't save time — it destroys productivity while creating the comforting illusion that unrealistic schedules are achievable.
